title: Remove Duplicates (in-place)
code: // LC 26 — Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array; returns new length k.
int slow = 0; // last index of the unique prefix (also its position)

frames.caption: Initialize slow = 0. nums[0] = 1 is the first (and so far only) unique value — the kept prefix is [0..0].
frames.note: Both pointers start at the front. slow is the index of the last unique value written so far (so the kept prefix is nums[0..slow]); fast will read every remaining element. We seed slow at 0 because the very first element is always unique by itself, then start scanning from fast = 1.

frames.caption: fast=1: nums[1] = 1 == nums[slow] = 1: duplicate, skip. slow stays 0, k stays 1.
frames.note: nums[fast] equals nums[slow], so this is a duplicate of the value already sitting at the tail of the unique prefix. We keep slow where it is and just advance fast — nothing is written. This is why the technique is O(1) space: duplicates are stepped over in place, never copied anywhere.

frames.caption: fast=2: nums[2] = 2 != nums[slow]: new value. slow becomes 1, write 2 -> nums[1]. k = 2.
frames.note: nums[fast] differs from nums[slow], so it is a brand-new value. We advance slow by one (opening the next prefix slot) and write nums[fast] there. Because the array is sorted, every distinct value appears as a contiguous run, so comparing only against nums[slow] — the last unique kept — is enough to detect the first occurrence of each new value.

frames.caption: fast=3: nums[3] = 2 == nums[slow] = 2: duplicate, skip. slow stays 1, k stays 2.
frames.note: nums[fast] equals nums[slow], so this is a duplicate of the value already sitting at the tail of the unique prefix. We keep slow where it is and just advance fast — nothing is written. This is why the technique is O(1) space: duplicates are stepped over in place, never copied anywhere.

frames.caption: fast=4: nums[4] = 3 != nums[slow]: new value. slow becomes 2, write 3 -> nums[2]. k = 3.
frames.note: nums[fast] differs from nums[slow], so it is a brand-new value. We advance slow by one (opening the next prefix slot) and write nums[fast] there. Because the array is sorted, every distinct value appears as a contiguous run, so comparing only against nums[slow] — the last unique kept — is enough to detect the first occurrence of each new value.

frames.caption: Done. Return slow + 1 = 3. The unique prefix is nums[0..2] = [1, 2, 3]; everything past index 2 is leftover garbage and is ignored.
frames.note: The answer is the length of the kept prefix, which is slow + 1 (slow is an index, so the count is one more). This is the classic off-by-one trap of compaction: LC 26 returns slow + 1 because slow is the index of the last unique element, whereas LC 27 (Remove Element) uses slow as a post-incremented write counter and returns slow directly.
